items.q: What is the first step of formal analysis?
items.correct: Description - noting what is actually present in the work.
items.why: Students jump to meaning immediately. Formal analysis follows a sequence: describe, analyse formal elements, interpret, evaluate. Description grounds interpretation in what's actually there.

items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.correct: Strong contrast between light and dark to model three-dimensional form.
items.why: Students think it's about subject matter (night scenes). Chiaroscuro is a TECHNIQUE using value contrast to suggest volume - exemplified by Caravaggio but used since the Renaissance to model form.

items.q: What do you add to make a tint?
items.why: Correct answer: White. “Black” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Monochrome means using how many colours?
items.why: It's One. “Two” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many ways to alter a colour are listed: tint, shade, tone?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
